title: Mixture (Fast Trap)
subject: math
summary: Mixture problems in SPTG usually test concentration, dilution, weighted average, and conservation of the main substance.
The algebra is not difficult, but many students lose points by averaging percentages incorrectly.
The most important idea is that the amount of pure substance is tracked, not just the total liquid.
coreConcepts: Amount of pure substance = concentration × volume.
When two solutions are mixed, total pure substance is the sum of the pure substance from each part.
Final concentration = total pure substance / total volume.
When water is added, the amount of pure substance stays the same but the total volume increases.
When some solution is removed, both pure substance and total volume are reduced proportionally.
Do not average concentrations directly unless the volumes are equal.
derive: If a solution has concentration c and volume v, then pure substance = cv.
If two solutions are mixed, total pure substance = c1v1 + c2v2.
Total volume after mixing = v1 + v2.
If only water is added, pure substance stays constant, so old concentration × old volume = new concentration × new volume.
formulas: final concentration = total pure substance / total volume
if x liters are removed from a uniform solution, the same fraction of pure substance is removed
traps: Do not take the simple average of two percentages unless the volumes are equal.
Adding water changes total volume but not pure substance.
If some liquid is removed first, the removed part contains both water and solute in the same proportion as the mixture.
Watch out for whether the question asks for final concentration, added water, or original volume.
Percent must be converted consistently in equations.

sptgSet.question: Mix 10 L of 20% solution with 10 L of 40% solution. Find the final concentration.
A 20% solution has volume 50 L. How much pure substance does it contain?
Mix 5 L of 10% solution with 15 L of 30% solution. Find the final concentration.
Mix 8 L of 25% solution with 12 L of 50% solution. Find the final concentration.
How much water must be added to 10 L of 40% solution to make it 20%?
Mix x L of 20% solution with 10 L of 50% solution to get 30%. Find x.
A 30% solution becomes 20% after adding 5 L of water. Find the original volume.
Mix 10 L of 10% solution with x L of 50% solution to get 30%. Find x.
Remove 5 L from 20 L of 40% solution and replace it with water. Find the new concentration.
Mix equal volumes of 20% and 60% solutions. Find the final concentration.

hardSet.question: Mix 10 L of 20% solution with 30 L of x% solution to get 35%. Find x.
A tank contains 40 L of 25% solution. If 10 L is removed and replaced with water, find the new concentration.
Mix 15 L of 30% solution with 25 L of 50% solution. Find the final concentration.
A solution is 60% alcohol. After adding 20 L of water, it becomes 30% alcohol. Find the original volume.
Remove 20% of a 50% solution and replace it with water. Repeat this process twice. Find the final concentration.
Two solutions have concentrations 10% and 40% and are mixed in the ratio 2:3. Find the final concentration.
A solution is diluted from 40% to 25% by adding 30 L of water. Find the original volume.
Two solutions have concentrations 20% and 80%. In what ratio should they be mixed to obtain 50%?
A container has 100 L of 30% solution. If 20 L is removed and replaced with 60% solution, find the new concentration.
Mix x L of 20% solution and y L of 60% solution to obtain 40%. Find the ratio x:y.
